speaker: interviewer
text: --Erna Florsheim, October 20, 1996. So you arrived in the United States. Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I arrived, I stayed with my cousin in Forest Hills, and they had a little boy. And I stayed with them for-- I believe it was for a few months. I was looking for work. The first thing we did, we wrote to Albany to see if I need a license to work here. I sent them a copy of my certificate that I received in England. And they replied that if I want to work in a hospital with newborn and well children, I don't need a special license. And then we went to the HIAS and asked for assistance to help me get a job. And I-- they got me a job at Women's Hospital on 110 Street in-- here in Manhattan. And I-- they interviewed me nice, showed them all the-- my papers, and everything was fine. And I started to work for them, and then I moved out from my cousin in Forest Hills. I had a small room on 112th Street with a family by the name of [? Oppenheimer. ?] They-- it was a very tiny room, but it was mine, and I was-- I was by myself. And-- and I only lived two blocks away from my work. I worked at Women's Hospital. I was approximately seven years working there, and I took care of newborn babies. Then later on, I was asked to-- in those days, you still made formulas and whatever the-- the pediatrician prescribed. And I-- I was running the formula room, as it was called, sterilize and so on. And I also taught new mothers how to take care of their babies, and I enjoyed that very much. I made $35 a week before taxes. I had-- I paid my room, which was $6 a week at the time, and I paid back my uncle the carfare, the-- the fare to come here to the country. So very often I was lucky if I had enough money for a sandwich a day. But in the meantime, Margot, who was here in the United States already, introduced me to my husband. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was engaged to my husband's cousin. As a matter of fact, when I was in England and I decided to come to the United States, she wrote to me, I have someone for you when you come here, and I would like you to meet him. So I wrote back to her, put him in mothballs until I get there. [LAUGHTER] And so she was here, and we got together again. And very often-- when she-- after she introduced me to Alfred, and somehow we liked each other from the beginning. And sometimes-- he didn't have much money. He was working, but also going to college at night. And he took me to the Automat too, and we sat there with coffee, for coffee, and we-- we talked. And once in a while, when he took me out for a dinner I couldn't eat. My stomach had shrunk, and I couldn't eat the-- because the perspiration was running down on me. I-- whether it was weakness or whatever, I don't know. I didn't have the money to buy food.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I always told him, I'm not hungry. Let's just go for coffee, and that's what we did. And only much later did I tell him the reason why I didn't want him to take me to eat, because I couldn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when-- that was-- I met him in 1948. We got engaged in the spring '49. September 1949, we got married. And we worked together. Very often, I used to do double shifts at the hospital to make a little extra money. And Alfred, he used to go after work to people's homes to repair radios, and he built his own television.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we were going out, I used to go Tuesday evenings to his house, to his parents, and we used to watch television there, because very few people had television in those days. And he built it, and he-- and I remember it was Milton Berle was on on Tuesday nights, and so we laughed. And then he used to-- to bring me home to 112 Street where I lived. And we worked together all these years. Was your husband born in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was born in Germany also. He came to this country in 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: With his parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: His parents came a year later in 1937. He has one brother who came at that time with his parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when do you feel you first felt well again? When did you feel healthy and comfortable?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took a long time. It took years. [SIGHS] I always felt [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was so much missing in my life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as my husband told me, said, he even says it to me now, I'm too serious. I take everything just very hard, and I can't ever relax and all this. I can't. It's in me. It's something that I grew up with, and I could never shake it. It's something that stay-- will stay with me always. It-- but I've been fortunate in many things, and I'm grateful for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember a time when you sat down to eat and were comfortable and could actually eat comfortably and not think about all the times that you couldn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh, yes. After we were married and it was a home-- we had, you know, I had a home, yes, I did enjoy it, and I did-- and even to this day. But I'm-- I-- I'm very grateful every day of my life, and I haven't forgotten. And I-- I will always be grateful for what I have now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we never had children. It-- I suppose it has something to do with the war years, that I could not have children.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you were not able to.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not able to, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you have to say about all that has happened to you and how it's affected your outlook on life, your feeling toward humanity?

speaker: interviewee
text: It affected me very much that my good years, all these years, to be deprived of my parents, deprived of my home, deprived of my freedom. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as far as I was thrown out of my home, and I have not been able to go back, and I don't think I ever will. This is a very deep feeling. Although I have been asked several times to come back for a visit and so on to see your old home, your house, and this. But I always decline because I don't think I could do it emotionally.

speaker: interviewer
text: With whom do you have contact in Konigsbach?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now it's a Professor Mehe, M-E-H-E. He is teaching history in Konigsbach at a university. When I grew up-- oh, but the few years I was there, there was no university there. They took a few towns together and made it one big city now, I suppose.

speaker: interviewee
text: And about one or two years ago, I received a letter from one of his students, where she writes that they heard that I survived the war. They would like to have some details. Could I tell them what happened to me? And I wrote them a few pages, legal-size pages, and I-- I told them I just give you a few highlights, and-- which I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And since that time, that Professor Mehr has called me several times from Germany. Only very recently, a few weeks ago, he called me and again invited me to come. And he also told me he will come to the United States next year, and he would like to meet me. I said I will be very happy to meet him. And he said he's teaching the history. And he also took some students to Aufhausen where my father came from, and-- and he went to the cemetery where my grandfather is buried. And I told him that my grandmother, who was close to 90 years old, was deported. And he said to me, no, she was not. They took her to Stuttgart, and she died there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he has documentation. He said he would send it to me. He also said he-- there was another book published not so long ago, and it's on its way here to me. And the first letter that I wrote to them is in that book. So I am waiting for the book to see what it is. Is he Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't believe so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you have contact with anyone there whom you actually knew or only with this professor?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is a-- a-- someone that went to school-- I went to school with in Konigsbach wrote to me, and I don't remember her at all. She says she remembers me. But I was in Konigsbach such a short time in school. And she remembers-- she wrote she remembers my mother, my father, my sister. And-- but I don't remember her name, or I cannot visualize her at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have contact with anyone here whom you knew from there besides Margot?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, last Sunday we were 15 people here, and we got together. These are all people from Pforzheim. And I'm actually from-- the only one from Konigsbach, because Margot, who used to live in Konigsbach, moved to Pforzheim before we were deported.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we met here, and we meet but once or twice a year in one or the other's house, and we have a very nice get-together always. We talk very rarely or very little about what happened to us. Some of my-- of these people had it a lot worse than I had. They were in Riga. They were-- went through an awful lot. And-- but we don't talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a good time together. We get together, and we-- most of them have children and grandchildren. So we look at the pictures, and we-- it's-- it's a very nice group and very congenial. And it's-- the husbands, we all get along well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever spoken about your experiences before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never have. I never could. This is really the first time that I really talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think has made it possible for you to talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother Sigi, who lives in Canada, he did-- he told his story just recently, a few weeks ago. And his son, his older son Jeffrey, who is a doctor in Toronto, says to me, always says to me, you have to do it for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was very hesitant, but I decided, OK, I will-- I'll try, and I'll do the best I can. And that's why I really decided to do it. And I-- I met-- I know someone from our synagogue, Mrs. [? Steinlauf, ?] and she told me also, you have to do it, and-- and so on. So with a little encouragement, I-- I did it. Is there anything that I haven't asked you that you'd like to talk about, or anything that you'd like to talk more about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really can't think of anything right now. I-- as I said, it-- no one should have to go through it. When some people say to me, it can-- it will not happen again, I'm not sure. I'm not as convinced as some people are. They say, yeah, in this country, it will not happen. And-- but from what I read in the paper every day, from what I see on television, I'm very much afraid. And I-- when some people say, not in our lifetime, but I don't-- wouldn't want it to happen in anybody's lifetime.

speaker: interviewer
text: In closing, is there a message that you would like to offer to future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: They should try and fight wherever they can to keep the heritage and never forget what happened before. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] To try, if it's in their power, to avoid, to-- to-- to work hard to-- [SIGHS] it's hard to how to put it-- to fight governments and people, to have compassion towards others.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Florsheim, would you introduce your husband to us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Alfred. I'm married to him for 47--

speaker: interviewee
text: --7 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: --years. And he's very kind. He's very good to me. And we have gone through rough times, also, through the years with-- mainly with sickness. And-- but thank god so far--

speaker: interviewee
text: So good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So far.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mr. Florsheim, how did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were introduced through, actually, relatives, you might say. And the first time I met her, that was it for me. I never had another date. We got married within a year of her arrival in the States. And as my wife said, we went through rough times, but the last few years we couldn't be without each other. I wouldn't know what to do without her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And vice versa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much of your wife's wartime experience did you know about, or do you know about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, only lately I got the rest of her experiences. She always was a little-- she didn't like to talk about it. It-- it make her too-- it brought tears to her eyes when she talked about her-- her parents especially. And in the beginning, I remember if a-- a couple planes went overhead, she tightened up. It was still in her from the wartime. Thank god, it's-- lately I don't think it-- it bothers her anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first learn about her wartime experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that I-- I heard about it in the beginning. I mean, I'm talking about details that I didn't know, the finer points or whatever you want to call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you feel that these experiences affected your relationship with one another?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- how shall I talk-- it made it, in the beginning, a little harder to get to know each other more closer, since she was always used to being on her own feet, for one thing. So it was hard for her to-- to listen to somebody else-- [LAUGHTER] --who thinks-- [LAUGHTER] --a little different. But we got along all right. We made it for 47 years. We'll make it for the rest of our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken when my parents were engaged. On the right is my father. On the left is my mother. This is a picture that I received from my uncle that lived in South America, and he send it to me when I came to America. And I made copies for my sister and two brothers and for myself. So this is the only real picture that we have, since we could not take anything along.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where the picture was photographed?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Pforzheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it by a studio or by--

speaker: interviewee
text: By a studio, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, what are the names of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Jakob Wassermann, and my mother's name Hannchen Wassermann. Her maiden name was Reutlinger. This picture was taken in 1933 in Konigsbach. On the right, that is me, Erna. Next to me is my brother Julius, my mother Hannchen, who is holding my youngest brother Sigi. And next to my mother is my sister Ilse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who took this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was an aunt that used to visit us, and she came from Pforzheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you obtain it or preserve it till now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I also received that from an uncle, also a brother of my mother who had several pictures-- this-- and gave it-- gave them to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these photographs were not actually in your possession in Germany. They were in the possession of relatives.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, yes. This is my father Jakob Wassermann. On the right, it's-- is me, Erna, and on the left is my brother [? Siggy. ?] I'm not exactly sure what year it was taken. It must have been about '35 or '36. It was taken in Konigsbach. I don't recall who took the picture. This is a postcard from France. It-- the place is called Vic-sur-Cere. And this building on the left is where I stayed for some time with some friends until we had to move on. This picture was taken here in New York. On the right is my sister Ilse, and I'm on the left. It was taken, I believe, soon after she came from Canada. She used to live in South America but wanted to come to the United States. And Alfred and I could not send her the-- the affidavit directly. So she had to stay for a short time in Canada before coming to-- to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your sister's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wassermann. This picture was taken at a function in May 1996. On the right is me, and on the left is my husband Alfred. This is a photograph of my brother Sigi and his family at the wedding of his oldest son Jeffrey. On the right is his son Trevor. Next to Trevor is Michelle, my niece, and now my new niece, Janice, next to her new husband Jeffrey, my sister-in-law Jacqueline, and my brother Sigi. This document [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I received from Rivesaltes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It states that I was taken out of Rivesaltes by the OSE because of I was [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] less than 16 years old, and I was permitted to travel to Leon. This is a travel paper that I received in Marseille in 1945 so I could continue traveling to Paris. This is a letter from the OSE stating that they took care of me during the war and that I was leaving on-- I was leaving for England in 1945. The International Red Cross informed me on June 1947 that they have no information regarding my parents' whereabouts. This is a book about Konigsbach and, among other things, about the Jews that lived there and the Jews that perished during the war. On page 280 and 281 are all the names listed of the people that perished during the war. On page 281 are my parents, Hannchen and Jakob Wassermann.
